data.title: Rhythm Pharmaceuticals: Exciting Times With sNDA PDUFA Date
data.description:
This is my third Rhythm (NASDAQ:RYTM) article, following 06/2025's "Rhythm Pharmaceuticals: Rare Genetic Obesity Pioneer With Excellent Prospects." In Excellent Prospects, I rated Rhythm as a "Buy." It has since enjoyed a tasty ~52% bump. In this article, I will assess its forward prospects to see if it is still an attractive investment candidate.

Rhythm is Motoring Ahead With Possible New Blockbuster Approval for IMCIVREE in 12/2025

IMCIVREE Existing Approvals

As reported in Excellent Prospects in 2020, IMCIVREE (setmelanotide) was approved to treat a variety of rare genetic obesity diseases. In 2022 it was approved to treat a more widely prevalent genetic obesity abnormality, Bardet-Biedl syndrome [BBS].

The panel below from Rhythm's 09/2025 website Presentation shows its estimates of the prevalence of IMCIVREE's currently approved indications:

Anticipated sNDA

In its 08/05/2025 Q2/2025 Financial Results and Business Update release, Rhythm listed the following near-term expected milestone:

Complete submissions of a supplemental New Drug Application to the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) and a Type II variation request to the European Medicines Agency [EMA] seeking approval for setmelanotide for the treatment of acquired hypothalamic obesity [AHO] in the third quarter of 2025;

Just a short ~2 weeks later, on 08/21/2025, Rhythm issued a release announcing:

  1. the FDA has accepted Rhythm’s supplemental New Drug Application [sNDA] for setmelanotide in the treatment of AHO;
  2. the FDA has granted priority review for the sNDA, assigning 12/20/2025 as the PDUFA date;
  3. the EMA took analogous action, with its review expected to begin on 08/16/2025.

AHO Potential

If Rhythm is able to snag required approvals for IMCIVREE to treat AHO, it will significantly grow its potential market reach, as shown by its Presentation panel below:

An FDA approval for AHO in 12/2025 would expand IMCIVREE's reach by a factor of 2X or 3X. It is this potential that undergirds Goldman Sachs' IMCIVREE peak revenue estimate of $1.9 billion and Jefferies' of up to $3 billion.

In response to an analyst question during the Call as to the anticipated launch characteristics for AHO and the markets it would approach, CEO Meeker provided a thoughtful response, noting:

... AHO, [has a] bigger epidemiology [than BBS]. I mean we've had questions this morning, and we'll get -- continue to get a lot of questions about how big could this be. I think where we are now, a lot of confidence in our current projections -- could it be bigger? Absolutely.

Will it have some of the same dynamics as these kind of ultra-rare diseases? And AHO sits a little bit in the middle. It's absolutely a rare disease, quite rare, 5,000 to 10,000 put it in the very rare category, but it's very specialty like given the concentration, more patients diagnosed, an attentive specialty by definition. So AHO may have a slightly different ramp, if you will, steeper than BBS because of those factors. But the other aspects of AHO are going to be very similar, which is think about steady growth over time.

Don't think about inflecting this is something and explode out of the gates and way some of these "specialty opportunities do." That's going to be more rare like, but it will be steeper and it will grow for a very long time. And we may be wrong in the epidemiology, meaning that it could be larger and given enough time, I think that's likely. ...

This anticipated long development path walks us right up to the next critical question—what kind of IP protection does IMCIVREE have? Unfortunately, the answer is not long enough.

Rhythm's Patent Extension Strategy is Riddled With Risks

Rhythm's website Presentation slide 10 below shows how Rhythm is making an all-out attack on AHO, not just with IMCIVREE (setmelanotide), but also with Bivamelagon, and RM-718:

This is a rare concentration of focus on a single indication. What gives?

Obviously, there are lots of rationale for attacking a particular disease with multiple molecules. It is always good to have a backup. Different molecules involve different potential interactions and have different modes of delivery:

  1. IMCIVREE—injected subcutaneously once daily;
  2. Bivamelagon—oral
  3. RM-718—injection with work on auto-injector in progress.

The issue, which I suspect is the biggest driver of focus on these two next-generation therapies, is their extended patent life. During the Call Meeker advised:

...Setmelanotide composition of matter patent is up in 2032, but importantly, the formulation patents extend to 2034 in the U.S. Our next-generation compounds will extend that protection to 2040 plus.

Given the extended path expected for full realization of AHO revenues, setmelanotide's modest remaining patent term is insufficient. In order for Bivamelagon and RM-718 to work as patent extenders, they will need to both:

  1. secure FDA approval to treat AHO with comparable or more favorable labels; and
  2. succeed in the marketplace against likely lower-priced IMCIVREE generics.

Rhythm's Liquidity Supports a Modest Cash Runway.

During the Call CFO Smith advised that Rhythm had cash, cash equivalents and short-term investments of $291 million as of 06/30/2025. He also advised that in 07/2025 Rhythm paid $40 million to LG Chem as part of its Bivamelagon acquisition obligation.

He went on to advise that:

...Rhythm's cash on hand, combined with the net proceeds from last month's stock offering, forecasted revenues from the anticipated launch of IMCIVREE acquired HO as well as ongoing revenue from approved indications and currently planned R&D and commercial activity provides cash runway of at least 24 months.

Rhythm does not issue any revenue guidance. A two-year runway dating from 06/30/2025 is not much of a runway. Unless its liquidity takes an unexpected positive move upward, I expect it to be looking for additional financing at some point in the next few quarters.

Its Q2/2025 10-Q lists the following menu of potential fundraising options:

...proceeds from the issuance of debt, sale of equity, proceeds from out license arrangements, product sales and funded research and development programs...

Conclusion

While I remain a staunch Rhythm bull, I do expect it to rest and to consolidate its gains through the balance of 2025 into Q1/2025. I am dropping my "Buy" rating to "Hold".

I consider the biggest risks on the plus side to be:

  1. an unexpected acceleration in revenues;
  2. an out-license deal that provides a significant liquidity boost without dilution.

I consider the biggest risk on the negative side would be for the FDA to issue a CRL.

data.title: RFIX: Long Rates With Leverage
data.description:
Thesis

The long end of the treasury curve is an interesting place, but also a high-duration place. High duration translates into volatility when it comes to bonds, and substantial gains or losses can be had. Suffice to look at a historic graph of the widely utilized iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLT):

In the past decade, TLT has seen substantial 'action,' with gains exceeding 25% during Covid when rates plummeted, and a massive -50% drawdown since, as long rates moved above 4%.

Now while some readers might be scared by such volatility, one might be surprised to find out there are funds that offer 2x or even 3x the return profile of TLT.

In today's article we are going to discuss an interesting fund from Simplify Asset Management that takes a leveraged position on the long end, namely the Simplify Bond Bull ETF (NYSEARCA:RFIX).

What does the fund do? A leveraged take on the long end.

Simplify always structures its funds in an interesting way, and RFIX is no different. The ETF uses swaptions (options on swaps) in order to create a synthetic leveraged position on the long end of the curve. We have covered the RFIX mirror fund before, namely the Simplify Interest Rate Hedge ETF (PFIX) which we discussed here. Both names use swaptions to create their desired payoff profiles. Traditional ETFs usually use total return swaps or swaps with large banks to get a defined payoff profile.

RFIX is designed to profit when long rates fall and volatility rises, and is a fairly new fund having IPO-ed in 2024. Some of the fund analytics are:

  • AUM: $0.12 bil
  • Expense ratio: 0.5%
  • Duration: 41 years

Its current holdings composition is as follows:

We can observe the name parking its cash in T-Bills, while having a number of swaptions and swaps with Morgan Stanley and Goldman Sachs.

The most interesting aspect is the result of this composition, and we shall explore that aspect in the next section.

Return profile

Via its composition, RFIX represents a leveraged take on the long end of the curve, and competes with several funds already in that space:

We are comparing RFIX here with the Direxion Daily 20+ Year Treasury Bull 3x Shares (TMF) and the ProShares Ultra 20+ Year Treasury (UBT) funds. While TMF states in its name the goals, UBT has the following as its objective - "seeks to replicate twice the daily performance of the ICE U.S. Treasury 20+ Year Bond Index, by using a full replication technique." So TMF is a 3x daily fund, while UBT is a 2x daily fund.

As we can see from the graph above, all three names had a similar performance up to June/July 2025, when TMF and UBT started to gain value, while RFIX plummeted. The reason? Volatility:

Remember that RFIX is long swaptions, thus has a vega and a delta component. While long rates did move lower, volatility also plummeted by almost 25% (from roughly 100 to 75 currently) as measured by the widely used bond volatility index 'MOVE':

While the VIX can help you prepare for stock market shifts—especially downward ones—the Merrill Lynch Option Volatility Estimate, commonly known as the MOVE Index (MOVE:GIF), tracks volatility in Treasuries to provide insight into the fixed income market and sometimes can even outpace the VIX as a red flag. Fixed income is the second largest market in the world behind currencies.

So RFIX needs both lower rates and higher or constant volatility in order to thrive. Since June 2025, it has underperformed its cohort due to much lower levels in volatility and the MOVE index.

At the end of the day, an investor putting money in RFIX is betting long rates are heading lower, and volatility is moving higher from current levels. We are going to show you in the next section why we believe the long end of the curve will stay elevated despite Fed Funds moving lower.

Fed funds are moving lower, but the long end is a different story

While many people are discussing the recent Fed cuts, investors need to realize that cutting Fed Funds does not mean that long dated yields will also come down. The Fed controls the front end of the yield curve, but the markets control longer durations:

In general, two-year Treasury yields can be seen as a compounding of two years' worth of daily federal funds rate expectations, with small adjustments for liquidity disparities and other factors. However, when it comes to 10-year Treasuries, factors like inflation, economic growth and Federal Reserve’s (Fed) monetary policy, among others, play important roles.

In fact, the market reaction to the recent Fed cut was the opposite of what some might have thought, with long yields moving higher:

Traders of longer-dated bonds “don’t want the Fed to be cutting interest rates,” Boockvar said. Their selling of long-term bonds drove down the price and drove up the yield. Prices and yields for bonds move in an inverse direction. Easing monetary policy at a time when inflation is running above the Fed’s 2% target and the economy looks steady can indicate the central bank is “taking the eye off” inflation, Boockvar said, a key risk to longer duration securities.

Unlike pandemic events like COVID, when the Fed engaged in quantitative easing to ease conditions in the economy, a slow and gradual move to a neutral Fed Funds rate might see just the front end of the yield curve move. And then we have the potential return of inflation, with the July 2025 PPI figures hitting a 3-year high:

While a resurgence of inflationary pressures is not guaranteed, the figures coming out of the economy coupled with tariff pressures are indeed very worrisome. Several research houses see the same scenario playing out:

With inflation elevated and fiscal deficits rising, there may not be much room for Treasury yields to fall much further, especially those with longer-term maturities. We look for a modest move down in short-to-intermediate term Treasury yields over the next few months as the Federal Reserve lowers the federal funds rate, but long-term yields are likely to remain elevated in the absence of falling inflation and/or a decline in fiscal deficits.

At the end of the day, it is about fiscal deficits and inflation. If these two are brought under control, there is scope for lower long rates. As of the writing of this article, there is no indication that either of the two are now fixed.

Conclusion

RFIX is an exchange-traded fund. The name represents a leveraged take on long rates, and makes money when 20-year rates move lower while volatility is also high. The ETF represents a similar payoff profile as TMF and UBT, but has the added component of volatility. RFIX has lost value this year as long rates have remained high and volatility has come down. We are of the opinion that the current fiscal deficit and inflationary pressures will keep the long end of the yield curve elevated, thus are a 'Sell' for RFIX.

data.title: Amtech Systems: Upgrading To Buy On Bullish AI-Related Equipment Revenue Growth
data.description:
Intro

Our last coverage on Amtech Systems, Inc. (NASDAQ:ASYS) was in July 2023, when we stamped a 'Hold' rating on the semiconductor-oriented material & equipment supplier. Shares were trading at $9.25 per share at the time. The company's keen valuation, positive bottom-line profitability, and strong balance sheet were all bullish indicators, although we awaited a breakout from an intermediate symmetrical triangle at the time before potentially putting capital to work on the long side. Usually, symmetrical triangles or coils represent a continuation pattern (bullish), but as we see below, this did NOT come to pass in late 2023.

In fact, ASYS stock stooped to well below $4 a share in early 2024 before finally making a lower low in April of this year. Suffice it to say, we reiterate that micro-cap stock investors should always wait until defined trends are in motion before investing capital dollars. As noted above, the lack of trading volume many times can nullify share-price action and adjoining technical patterns, especially when buying or indeed selling in this case at an accelerated clip. Therefore, irrespective of the spectacular gains ASYS stock has made since those April lows earlier this year, shares are still down roughly 3% since our commentary roughly 27 months ago and are not coming up against significant overhead resistance, as we see below.

Long-term Chart: Better Read on Investor Psychology

Therefore, given the below-average trading volumes in these plays, it always makes sense to consult long-term charts, as short-term rallies (like the stock has experienced in recent months) can be emotionally driven, all things remaining equal. To get a better perspective of how ASYS investors view this stock, we see from the long-term chart below that shares have basically traded in a range-bound manner for over a decade. This gives further credence to what we alluded to earlier in that ASYS stock (given the strength of the current multi-month rally) could rally to something like $12 over the near term, but anything beyond this point remains an uncertainty at this stage (remember: investor psychology tends not to change over the long term).

Near-term Fundamentals Improving

The company's third quarter of the present fiscal year (Q3 earnings announced 8/9/2025) has been the most near-term catalyst for the present trending move. Amtech's Q3 revenues of $19.6 million came in well above expectations, growing by 26% over the first quarter. What was encouraging, however, from a fundamental standpoint, is that both the 'Thermal Processing Solutions' segment as well as 'Semiconductor Fabrication' revenues surprised to the upside, resulting in much improved investor sentiment and share-price gains to boot. AI infrastructure-related spending continues to pay dividends for Amtech in Thermal Processing. Furthermore, the expansion of recurring revenue is taking place alongside growing capital equipment sales, as we learned from the CEPO below.

Related to revenue mix, we generated about 60% of our revenue from capital equipment and 40% from recurring revenue, including consumables, parts, and services. The balance between capital equipment and recurring revenue is important and reflects our strategy to expand recurring revenue streams while fully capitalizing on opportunities for equipment used to expand AI infrastructure. As we look ahead, our third-quarter bookings suggest we should continue to see strength for AI-related equipment revenue.

In the Semiconductor Fabrication Solutions segment, while weak mature node demand persisted, Q3 results came in better than expected, especially in the consumables area. Suffice it to say, although Amtech has been becoming a smaller outfit, management believes that plenty of value can be created through robust AI-related demand, operational discipline, and focused investments in the likes of semiconductor packaging equipment and applications alike in the Semiconductor Fabrication Solutions segment.

Favourable Gross Margin Trends

Amtech's gross profit of $9.1 million in Q3 benefited from an employee-related refund to the tune of $1 million. However, even if we exclude the employee retention credit, gross margin for the quarter topped 41%, which was an excellent readout compared to the same quarter of 12 months previous (36.5%). The above trend enabled the company to report a Q3 operating profit of $1.4 million (compared to $0.9 million in Q3 of fiscal 2024), despite the fact that revenues in Q3 came in $7.1 million shy of their year-over-year comparable.

Suffice it to say, when a company can downsize the number of manufacturing sites it runs and yet keep on growing its margins and profitability, it speaks volumes for the stock concerned. Furthermore, a rising gross margin metric can act as an excellent hedge against rising inflation over time. Since margin gains usually maintain themselves down the income statement, it boils down to how much more gains can be extracted over time. Management did point to the potential for further savings from subletting unutilized facilities and the fact that recent cost reduction initiatives have yet to hit the income statement (further operational-led margin gains expected).

Revenues May Remain Lumpy, but Revisions Remain Encouraging

Therefore, given how strong AI equipment bookings are expected to offset muted node product demand to some degree, we will be keeping an eye on how margins fare out, as the timing of orders/shipments may affect top-line results quarterly. However, on an annual basis, forward-looking revisions look favourable with upward revisions for both revenues and earnings outnumbering downward revisions.

Conclusion

Therefore, given Amtech's strong multi-month rally, growing margins, and better-than-expected top-line results, we are upgrading the stock to a 'Buy' until further notice. Amtech stock looks like it has further to run here, where a move to the $12 level would return over 23% for qualifying shareholders. We look forward to continued coverage.

data.title: Corning: A Great Growth Plan Doesn't Make This A 'Buy'
data.description:
One company that I find to be very interesting is Corning (NYSE:GLW). But sadly, it's a firm that I don't write about very often. In fact, I haven't written about the company since I rated it a ‘hold’ back in April of 2021. At the time, I discussed how there were strong signs of a turnaround but that shares of the company were expensive. Since then, we have seen some interesting results. Even though top-line figures have gotten better very recently, revenue has a history of being lumpy. On top of this, profitability has gradually declined. But this year, things are looking up. In light of this volatility, and in spite of strong expectations for this year, I believe that the business deserves a ‘hold’ rating.

Digging into Corning

If you go back in time, you will find that very few companies have roots as old and deep as what Corning has. Although the company was incorporated in 1936, it actually began as a simple glass business back in 1851. From those humble origins, it has grown to become a major and diverse enterprise that caters to the technology industry. This includes its glass science, ceramic science, and optical physics areas of focus. The company has achieved this through significant research and development initiatives that have created tremendous shareholder value and built a business with a market capitalization today of $68.20 billion. Operationally speaking, the firm is massive, with manufacturing operations spread across 124 plants in 15 countries.

To really understand the business, we should break it up into its five operating segments. The largest of these, based on revenue, is the Optical Communications segment. This business is really broken up into two separate groups. One of these would be the carrier network group that caters to the optical-based communications infrastructure industry, such as those that facilitate video services, data and voice services, and communications. The other would be its enterprise network group that provides optical-based communication networks that are sold to businesses, governments, and individuals. A lot of what this segment does centers around optical fiber products, but it also includes cable assemblies, fiber optic hardware, closures, network interface devices, and more.

Next in line, we have the Display segment. This part of the company produces glass substrates for flat panel displays like televisions and computer monitors. The Specialty Materials segment produces items using different formulations for glass, glass ceramics, and crystals that are used in special hardware like metrology instruments. It also offers up software that caters to this market. Some of the work this segment does has been with Apple (AAPL), with the company specifically working to deliver durable glass with infused colors for the back of iPhones. And on top of this, it engages in the semiconductor optics space.

The company also has a segment dedicated to automotive products aptly called the Automotive segment that produces 'ceramic substrates and filter products for emissions control in mobile applications.' These are used in the production of catalytic converters. It also produces other related items that go into catalyzers, certain engines, emission control systems, and more. It is worth noting that, prior to this year, this was known as the Environmental Technologies segment. But starting in January of 2025, management put its Automotive Glass Solutions business into this unit and changed it to the Automotive segment we know today. And finally, we have the Life Sciences segment, which produces laboratory products such as plastic vessels, liquid handling plastics, cell culture media, glassware, and more.

At the end of the day, I consider this set of operations to be fascinating. But in recent years, there has been some volatility in the company's overall financial performance. Revenue, for instance, dropped from $14.19 billion in 2022 to $12.59 billion in 2023. But then, in 2024, there was an increase to $13.12 billion. It is important to be mindful, however, that there is a lot going on here. Yes, there have been weak spots for some of its operating segments. Over the last three years, for instance, the Life Sciences segment reported a plunge in revenue from $1.23 billion to $979 million. Management blamed this on lower demand for COVID-19-related products in China and the fact that customers in North America and Europe were drawing down on inventories. The inventory drawdown isn't really shocking to me. Supply chain issues caused significant inventory investments to be made as COVID-19 became a relic of the past. So some degree of normalization, not only in this space but in many other industries, is to be expected.

To be honest with you, all the company's operating segments, with the exception of its Display segment, reported meaningful volatility over the past few years. The Optical Communications segment, as an example, saw revenue plummet from $5.02 billion in 2022 to $4.01 billion in 2023. But then, in 2024, there was a partial recovery to $4.66 billion. The original decline from 2022 to 2023 was driven by lower order rates from carriers as they focused on drawing down their inventories. But as investments in AI-related connectivity solutions that are instrumental in the construction of data centers started to grow, this segment saw a nice return to growth.

The Specialty Materials segment for the company has experienced volatility as well. But the good news is that, after seeing revenue drop from $2 billion in 2022 to $1.87 billion in 2023, there was a rebound to $2.02 billion last year. This was thanks to strong demand for premium glass that's used in mobile devices. Semiconductor-related sales also saw strength.

In the Automotive segment, we have seen some mixed results as well, with revenue initially rising from $1.58 billion in 2022 to $1.77 billion in 2023. It then fell to $1.67 billion last year, with management blaming this on a weaker global heavy-duty diesel market. This is not shocking to me in the least. For starters, a continued shift to electric vehicles will weigh on this unit. But in addition to this, weaker economic activity has proven to be painful for the heavy-duty market specifically. That includes not only in the US but in Europe as well, which management specifically pointed out as a weak area.

Then we have the Display segment, which has been the true growth juggernaut of the business in recent years. From 2022 through 2024, its revenue has risen consistently, climbing from $3.31 billion to $3.87 billion. Management claims that this has been the result of greater demand for larger average screen sizes as well as higher prices that they have been able to put onto customers.

Interestingly, if we add all of these segments together, we would actually see that revenue for the business has been flatter than what the top line figures show. Yes, there was a drop from $13.14 billion across these segments in 2022 to $12.13 billion in 2023. But then, there was a nice recovery to $13.19 billion last year. More problematic for the company has been foreign currency fluctuations, which went from negatively affecting the company by $616 million in 2022 to $1.31 billion last year. And a lot of this pain came from the Display segment that has otherwise performed so well.

On top of this, there is another unit worth mentioning. But it's not considered one of its core operating segments. This is the Hemlock and Emerging Growth Businesses that the company has. This is essentially a catchall for other businesses that don't fit under its individual operating segments. Examples here include its Hemlock Semiconductor Group that provides high-purity polysilicon products for the solar power and electronics industries, as well as its pharmaceutical technologies business. Its revenue has contracted from $1.66 billion in 2022 to $1.28 billion last year. And according to management, this has been the result of lower volumes and lower pricing related to solar-grade polysilicon.

Even more problematic for the company in recent years has been its bottom line. From 2022 through 2024, net income plummeted from $1.32 billion to $506 million. This primarily stems from the fact that, relative to 2022, selling, general, and administrative costs remained relatively flat while research and development costs increased. Other profitability metrics have been largely weaker also. Operating cash flow dropped from $2.62 billion to $1.94 billion. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a decline from $2.61 billion to $2.28 billion. And finally, EBITDA for the company fell from $3.29 billion to $2.74 billion.

When it comes to this year, however, the picture is looking much better. Overall revenue for the company grew from $6.23 billion in the first half of 2024 to $7.31 billion in the first half of this year. The primary driver of this, by far, has been its Optical Communications segment, with revenue skyrocketing from $2.04 billion to $2.92 billion. According to management, strong demand for generative AI products, as well as for telecommunications investments, with the former pushing up demand for data center interconnect products and the latter pushing up demand for fiber-to-the-home products, can be thanked for this.

Outside of this, top-line results were a bit disappointing. Display revenue, for instance, dipped from $1.89 billion to $1.80 billion. This was primarily because of lower sales volumes as panel makers reduced their utilization in the face of weaker demand. Specialty Materials revenue did increase from $955 million to $1.05 billion, with that growth attributable to strong demand for premium glass that is used in mobile devices. We saw a decline in Automotive revenue from $970 million to $900 million. This was caused by weakness in both the light-duty and heavy-duty markets in Europe and North America. Life Sciences revenue remained almost flat, dipping from $485 million to $484 million. And when it came to the Hemlock and Emerging Growth Businesses operations of the company, greater demand pushed revenue up from $523 million to $570 million.

The surge in revenue that the company reported brought with it a surge in profitability as well. Net income doubled from $313 million to $626 million. We saw operating cash flow jump from $617 million to $859 million. But that's not all. Even if we strip out changes in working capital, we would get a nice surge from $1.07 billion to $1.47 billion. And finally, EBITDA for the business expanded nicely from $1.26 billion to $1.69 billion.

I think it's important to note that this recovery in sales is not merely a coincidence. Yes, the company has catalysts it is benefiting from. However, management has done this in a calculated manner. In the final quarter of 2023, the firm launched what it calls its Springboard plan aimed at significantly growing revenue and boosting profit margins. In fact, when the company announced this initiative, it claimed that it would be able to grow revenue, from 2023 through the end of 2026, by at least $3 billion. However, the firm's target internally was $5 billion. Its high confidence plan has now been boosted to $4 billion, with its internal plan growing to $6 billion. This growth is possible by the company targeting certain opportunities like generative AI, data center demand, growing investments in connectivity, optical communications investments, and more. And it involves the business focusing all of its efforts on the things that it does uniquely well. Clearly, this strategy is starting to bear fruit.

Even though the overall trajectory for the business is positive in this regard, I think it's difficult to be terribly optimistic. In the chart above, you can see how the company is valued based on results from 2024 and estimates for 2025. The 2025 data assumes that the second half of this year looks like the first half of the year did on a year-over-year basis. However, the chart also shows the company under the assumption that it hits its targets at the high end of the range. The increase in revenue was based on annualized revenue figures from the final quarter of 2023. This would give us overall sales, if we add $6 billion to that, of $17.98 billion. Assuming the company captures the same kind of profit margins that it achieved in 2022, this would mean profits of about $1.67 billion, adjusted operating cash flow of $3.31 billion, and EBITDA of $4.17 billion. Even with this factored in, the firm looks to be pretty solidly fairly valued to me.

Company Price / Earnings Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Corning - 2025 67.4 21.9 20.4
Corning - Q4 2026 Forecast 40.9 20.6 16.4
TDK Corporation (OTCPK:TTDKY) 27.2 9.5 10.8
Kyocera Corporation (OTCPK:KYOCY) 115.1 12.0 15.9
Coherent Corp. (COHR) 90.0 26.9 19.9
Littelfuse (LFUS) 54.7 16.8 14.8
OMRON Corporation (OTCPK:OMRNY) 555.0 12.6 11.9

In the table above, I then compared Corning to five other similar firms. If we use the 2025 estimates, we can see that two of the five companies are cheaper than it is on a price-to-earnings basis. On a price-to-operating cash flow basis, four of them are cheaper. And when it comes down to the EV-to-EBITDA approach, our candidate is the most expensive of the group. Not much changes if we assume that my estimates are correct for the future. The relative position of the company on a price-to-operating cash flow basis remains unchanged. Only one of the firms is cheaper than it is on a price-to-earnings basis. And four of the five are still cheaper when it comes to the EV-to-EBITDA approach.

Takeaway

Based on all the data currently available, I do believe that while Corning remains an interesting company, I don't think it's cheap enough to justify a bullish outlook. The firm looks pretty solidly fairly valued to me. So because of that, I think that maintaining it as a ‘hold’ candidate is the right choice.

data.title: Bladex: Growth Set To Continue With Fresh Capital Increase
data.description:
I have covered Banco Latinoamericano de Comercio Exterior (NYSE:BLX), or Bladex, many times. I currently give them a "hold" rating, which is not only talk. I also walk with Bladex as the second largest position in my portfolio and an important source of liquidity through the +5% (and growing) dividend.

I have not added to the position at recent prices but am happy to see the continued progress of earnings and capital formation. My rating is based on seeing no discount to intrinsic value at 1.25x book.

Bladex is not a compounder like many others I write about; it may have a slight moat given their specialized lending segment, but I would initiate sales at around 1.5x book if it ever gets there. If not, I'm happy to continue to hold it and continue to cash the dividend checks.

I discuss their business in greater detail here, here, and here. They function as a specialized trade finance lender to large corporations, commercial banks, and sovereign entities in all of Latin America. The short-term nature of their book allows for great agility in moving funds where spreads warrant and provides for an unusually liquid and safer bank than would appear at first glance.

Recent Performance:

From the most recent article (the third "here" in the paragraph above):

Those Q2 earnings are due the first week of August. Save for any incremental loan loss reserves (which we haven't seen in a while); expect earnings to remain around $50 million for the quarter and an ROE of over 14%. Capital should grow again, as dividend payments are tracking well behind earnings at just $23 million per quarter ($0.625 x 37 million shares).

Results came in ahead of my expectation at $64 million and a (likely peak) ROE of 18%. Most of that was driven by extraordinary fee income, driven by an unusually large structuring fee on behalf of Staatsolie - Suriname's national oil company. The almost $20 million in quarterly fees will prove to be a peak, at least in the medium term, while they ramp up the new Letter-of-Credit platform.

Seeing steady growth in their loan book beyond prior records, I did not expect to see what came next. In September, the bank issued a specially designed security to increase their capital account. Given the strong position at the bank, such an issue is likely timed to fund additional growth (the balance sheet does not need repairing, and rating outlooks are all positive).

I will provide more details on the notes issued further on, but first, let's review how the bank has already pursued growth.

The Capital Position:

Bladex’s strong earnings have allowed the bank to build capital the “old-fashioned way,” by retaining a portion of each year’s income. The resulting dividend payout ratio—below 50% - shows the bank is paying out only a proportion of the total amount available for distribution in order to support capital formation and allow for continued asset growth.

Assets have indeed grown to levels well beyond the bank's prior peaks - a fact that needs to be appreciated in light of the near-perfect underwriting record. There have been no write-downs in the last three years - a streak that will undoubtedly end soon but is impressive nevertheless.

Since interest rates lifted from zero, the bank has been growing its capital by about $100 million per year. Each dollar in capital turns into $8 or $9 in assets. Up until this month, the bank appeared content to grow capital through retained earnings and grow at the speed limit imposed by internally generated capital.

I should point out that rating agencies routinely point to Bladex’s relatively low liquidity ratio, relatively high capital-market funding, and lower capital position as limiting factors to better scores (in the chart below, all three of the "larger grey bars" should be closer to the "green line"):

I disagree with rating agencies on all three counts. Bladex is unique in their short maturity loan profile, which means the bank is able to raise cash quickly by letting loans mature and disbursing new ones only as liquidity allows. This characteristic is not present in traditional banks, which hold longer-term loans that include mortgages, auto, credit cards, along with corporate loans and project financing. About 2/3 of loans at Bladex become cash in any 12-month period.

In short, Bladex is less susceptible to liquidity risk—their “deposits” are exclusively time deposits. Should capital markets close, Bladex can make it up by shrinking the book as long as required. This is what happened in the first half of 2020, with no single client entering into difficulty despite aggressive lockdowns in LatAm. Today, we are on the opposite side of this, with credit spreads tight and ample liquidity.

The recent capital raise is a clear sign that the opportunity pipeline is larger than the bank’s ability to fund without tapping outside sources. For those of us looking for signs that growth is set to continue, the transaction is nothing but good news. The transaction's terms and conditions are further "evidence" that funding conditions are favorable in the region.

The Subordinated Bond Issue:

The first thing to understand is that the instrument - although booked as equity - is not dilutive to current shareholders. There are no convertible features or even fixed payment accruals.

Here are some characteristics of the ATH capital issue:

  • $200 million of tier-1 capital was raised
  • at a cost of a 7.5% coupon
  • The notes are subordinated to all senior obligations of the bank (hence quasi capital).
  • The notes are perpetual (no maturity date—also good for booking them as capital instruments).
  • Bladex has the right to call the notes after seven years.
  • The coupon is non-cumulative, meaning if the bank can’t pay in a certain period, there is no carryover to future periods (also a capital-like feature).
  • The notes can be written off in certain events related to reorganization (a plus for debt holders and ratings—not so much for the equity holder, who would be wiped out by then).

In summary, the instruments carry very little risk to Bladex. The notes may never be paid back, and the coupon would not accumulate if missed. The only risk (and likely the reason there was so much demand for them) is that rates can go much lower and the 7.5% coupon would look expensive—in that case, there is no way out; the notes can be called but only until 2032.

This is not symmetric. If interest rates go up, the holders have no right to put the notes to Bladex and would have to feel satisfied with the coupon (theoretically in perpetuity).

For the time being, the bank is earning around mid-teens on its capital. At first sight, clearing the 7.5% hurdle related to this capital should be easy. However, we need to consider that what matters now is how much they can earn on incremental loans - meaning those funded by the new capital. In other words, the next $1.5B or so of assets that will come in in the next few quarters as a result of the strengthened capital position.

Risks:

Bladex is one of the most geographically diversified banks in Latin America. It is also one of the least diversified by “product” or “client type.” Due to their “concentration” of unsecured trade financing for corporations and financials throughout the region, it is both very concentrated and very diversified.

Things that affect the largest corporations in LatAm will affect Bladex. These are items such as the level of commodity prices and the level of currency, macroeconomic and political stability in the region. Bladex is not a new player; having more than 40 years of experience in the region, they have seen it all. Having central bankers from the region as shareholders and directors also helps.

In the past 10 years, and especially since the pandemic, the number of investment-grade countries in the region has been reduced. This represents an advantage for Bladex, because their investment-grade rating allows for efficient funding and wider margins when lending to non-IG credits. But those higher spreads are representative of higher risk. As usual, diversification is the key mitigation, as no country represents more than 12% of exposure typically.

Finally, the big tailwind from higher short-term US$ rates is easing. Slightly lower rates should spur growth and projects in the relevant markets; however, should rates move significantly lower (implying material US weakness), earnings power will be impacted at Bladex.

Valuation:

As noted at the beginning, the "hold" rating is based on valuation being "fair" rather than attractive. The discount to book value that persisted for many years has been erased, and it is now trading at a 25% premium. Despite this, it is still significantly cheaper on a relative basis.

Paying slight premiums to book is ok if returns on equity are also above what the market is expected to return long term. All of them are earning very good ROE, as they should in this part of the cycle. Bladex is amongst the lowest, which is to be expected given their conservative book (with extremely short duration and no direct consumer banking exposure).

All in, Bladex continues to offer a decent ROE at a reasonable price. Their dividend has become increasingly more affordable as earnings comfortably outpace dividend growth. As noted in a prior piece, their book in Argentina is already growing (mainly through quasi-sovereign exposures), and should other economies (like Venezuela) rejoin the global market, important opportunities would open up.

Summary:

As the stock increases in line with intrinsic value, there is no reason to change the current rating of "hold." The bank continues to grow and take advantage of favorable funding opportunities. Most recently, executing a capital strengthening AT1 transaction that will allow for total assets to grow near $15 Billion by next year - should conditions remain favorable in LatAm.

The multiple to book has expanded to 1.25x, still well below peers but also ahead of recent ranges for Bladex.

All considered, still a great bank to own, with an attractive and growing dividend yield supported by a low-risk and expanding book of business.

data.title: Rates Spark: Risks Of Complacency
data.description:
By Benjamin Schroeder & Michiel Tukker

Current markets display tight ranges and a very benign risk outlook

German Ifo data came in quite disappointing, which was a surprise given the backdrop of huge spending plans. Admittedly, the politics and practicalities around the implementation of the investment and reform programme remain challenging.

Earlier in the week, we also had some disappointment with the French PMI data. One could argue it is a reflection of the political challenges. In previous sessions, French government bond spreads over Bunds have continued to drift wider.

But overall, the market remains surprisingly steady - for instance, the pricing of the European Central Bank. The market is still indicating a roughly 40% chance of a rate cut over the next year. The 2y Schatz yield accordingly hovers just above 2%.

Further out the curve, the 10y Bund yield has hovered in a tight range around 2.75% over the past few days. 10y Bunds have become slightly more expensive versus swaps, but they still trade around 4bp above the swap rate, reflecting an overall relatively benign attitude towards risk. We also see many market indicators that point to very little risk ahead; implied volatility measures for both equities and rates are very low.

We think it is fair to ask whether markets are too complacent in the face of multiple risks. It seems markets have become accustomed to the political turmoil in France, although nothing has been resolved yet. And there is a continued insensitivity towards geopolitical developments. Even as some of the Russian incursions into NATO airspace appear to become more daring and the response from NATO becomes more tense.

For now, the appearance of complacency might be due to the notion that we are waiting for the next cues from the US, where the 10y Treasury is also little changed, waiting for more data to determine direction.

We still see the bearish direction of euro swap rates as our baseline scenario, but we also see plenty of risks that could quickly turn that narrative. Our baseline view of the ECB not cutting rates rests on the condition of growth continuing to improve, which is still a fragile assumption. As such, we still see good value in risk hedges, especially with shorter-dated German government bonds.

Thursday’s events and market view

The main events of the day are US data releases and Fed speakers. We will get the third and final reading of second-quarter GDP data, but more contemporaneous developments will be reflected in August durable goods orders and especially the weekly initial claims data. Fed speakers include those from both ends of the hawk/dove spectrum - from Schmid and Logan on the hawkish end, to Daly and Barr in the middle ground, to Williams, Bowman and Goolsbee on the dovish end.

In primary markets, the UK is tendering 9y and 13y gilts for up to £2bn in total. Later that day, the US Treasury will sell US$44bn in new 7y notes.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: Strategic Education: Growth To Accelerate In 2026 Following Temporary Headwinds
data.description:
The Thesis

Strategic Education (NASDAQ:STRA) boasts a high-quality management team with a lengthy tenure at the helm, strong higher education institutional assets, and a strategy that, when executed effectively, could fuel modest single-digit revenue growth. Historically, the stock has traded at a premium valuation compared to its for-profit postsecondary peers, often requiring investors to seize opportunities like earnings misses to accumulate shares amid business challenges.

I assume that at one time or another most writers on Seeking Alpha quote Warren Buffett to further the case of their stock pick. As it relates to STRA, the most relevant quote is the following: "The best thing that happens to us is when a great company gets into temporary trouble... We want to buy them when they're on the operating table."

STRA fits this mold, as it definitely has issues (which we believe to be temporary). The company has had some misses relative to consensus estimates as of late, including in Q4 2024 and Q2 2025. This has resulted in valuation multiple compression compared to its historical premium among for-profit postsecondary peers. The company isn't exactly on the operating table, but it has had to make some operational adjustments.

The primary headwind facing the company relates to the reported enrollment weakness in its Australia/New Zealand (ANZ) segment due to enrollment caps imposed by the Australian government. Ministerial Direction 111 (MD111), issued by Australia’s popular Labor government, effectively capped international student visas in 2025, impacting STRA's Torrens University, where roughly half the enrollments are international. This resulted in a material YoY decline in international enrollments for the past two quarters.

Management pivoted its recruitment efforts toward domestic market student acquisition. Enrollment declines in ANZ were modest in Q1 at 0.6% year-over-year but worsened to 3.1% in Q2, raising questions about the magnitude of potentially further declines in 2H25. Without quarterly guidance from management, investors are left uncertain about the magnitude of the Q3 and Q4 enrollment deterioration.

It's also worth mentioning that the company in 2025 dealt with tough YoY comps in its US Higher Education (USHE) segment. The company saw 5% revenue growth in 2024, but on a quarterly basis that growth decelerated throughout the year, leading to flat or declining revenue over the past three quarters. The decline was driven by non-employer-affiliated enrollments at Strayer University. These challenges potentially are nearing an end as tougher comparisons are lapped.

Given the temporary nature of the YoY declines in ANZ and the success of management's pivot to domestic student recruitment, I believe that growth can resume at STRA in 2026. Certainly at a greater level than the consensus revenue growth estimate for 2026 of 4% YoY (assuming current economic conditions). Given the overall frothiness of the US stock market, now may be a good time to begin accumulating a position. STRA's current forward P/E of 13.8x is 40% below the S&P 500’s 23x.

Naturally the question arises, isn't the Street already aware of how current issues may resolve? Why isn't this already priced into the stock?

Back when I was an analyst covering this stock 15 years ago, there were more than 20 sell-side investment banks covering this stock. Now there are only three estimates for forward revenue and EPS. This stock is simply underfollowed and lacks awareness from most investors.

But it's not just that -- risk does exist in buying shares today rather than waiting a quarter or two, namely that Q3 could see another miss relative to consensus estimates given challenges at ANZ. Why buy this stock now? Why not wait a quarter? These are fair questions. I've only established a modest position with the goal of potentially buying more shares after Q3 results.

My target price of $125 per share, based on a 20x P/E multiple (still a discount to the market), suggests nearly 50% upside.

Why I Like The STRA Story

I've always had a soft spot for Strayer University, one of the two institutions in the USHE segment, since I attended one of their graduation ceremonies back in 2010. As a working professional in the Washington, DC area, I knew several co-workers who had received their master's degrees from Strayer. Anecdotally, the institution has a good brand, at least in the DC area, and provides a valuable service to working adults. Obviously personal affinity isn't enough to invest in a company.

I like STRA because it has an actual strategy, with reinforcing activities that create differentiation in the highly competitive US higher education market. The company has a strategy to integrate itself into an enterprise through technology and processes. This in turn drives enrollments to its institutions.

Integration into the enterprise has always been the holy grail for for-profit universities. In practice, institutions acquire students from Internet lead generation like lead aggregators, targeted digital advertising on Facebook/Instagram/YouTube, and search engine optimization. Institutions employing these methods are effectively commodities and have no distinct competitive advantage. As student acquisition costs increase, margins go down.

STRA's Workforce Edge platform (found in the company's Education Technology Services, or ETS, segment) is a game changer, offering a comprehensive education tuition benefits program to employers. These programs focus on tuition assistance, reimbursement, and discounts to support upskilling, career mobility, and talent retention. The core online platform is provided at no cost, with the employers funding tuition reimbursements for their employees. Workforce Edge offers STRA's two US universities, Strayer University and Capella University, as options to employees and thus drives enrollments.

In 2024, employer-affiliated enrollment reached 29.6% of USHE’s total student enrollment, up from 27.2% in 2023, fueled by partnerships with over 1,300 employers offering tailored benefits. 30% obviously isn't enough to create a competitive moat. I would like to see this number rise closer to 50% over the next several years.

The chart below from the 2023 Investor Day explains how ETS revenue amplifies STRA USHE enrollments and revenue. The premise is that the corporate tuition benefits program will veer corporate employees toward STRA’s Strayer University and Capella University.

The 2023 Investor Day also introduced a “notional model” outlining long-term operational and financial goals across USHE, ANZ, and ETS, projecting steady revenue growth through employer-driven enrollment. The model is not formal guidance but management’s outline for revenue and earnings growth.

It would be ideal if the company could consistently execute annually within the range of the notional model. Unfortunately, variability does exist in higher education enrollments driven by market demand and regulatory constraints. Some years the company will overdeliver, like in 2024 with 7.7% YoY revenue growth, while in other years the company will underdeliver, like apparently in 2025 with an expected 3.5% YoY revenue growth. It's clearly better to buy the stock during a cyclical low, like what investors may be seeing right now.

No evidence exists that would suggest a deviation from the notional model reflecting either a structural change in the business or reduced demand for the company's offerings.

Street Estimates Reset Following Weak Q2, But Is It Enough?

STRA missed consensus revenue estimates by $1.4 million in Q2 2025, following a miss in Q4 2024. Over the past five years, without management guidance, Street estimates have swung between overestimating (2020-2022) and underestimating (2023-2024) revenue, leaving uncertainty about whether recent estimate revisions allow for beat-and-raise quarters.

The consensus estimate for Q3 revenue is $315M, a 3.1% YoY revenue growth rate. This represents an acceleration of revenue growth from 2.9% YoY growth. I have little insight as to why the sell-side modeled in revenue accelerating, particularly following a revenue miss in Q2. This admittedly is a concern going into the Q3 print. Below I have attempted to model whether a 3Q revenue estimate of $315M is realistic. It's interesting to note that it's eminently reasonable to think that STRA could meet or even exceed consensus revenue estimates. The $315M Q3 revenue estimate can be achieved if: 1) USHE sees flat YoY growth; 2) ANZ declines by 4% YoY; and 3) ETS revenue growth decelerates slightly.

On the surface, the consensus revenue estimate doesn't appear to be overly aggressive. Given the company's valuation, that's good enough for starting a new position.

Unpacking the Temporary Enrollment Challenges within ANZ

The Australian government put visa caps on international students through Ministerial Direction 111 on all Australian institutions of higher ed. The Australian government did this in response to housing shortages, rising rent inflation, and overcrowded classrooms following a post-pandemic surge of international students. This was not an assault on for-profit education like we've seen under certain US presidential administrations but rather a broader concern within all of higher ed within Australia.

Fortunately for investors, the Australian government is transparent on allotments for institutions of higher education for student visa applications. Torrens University (which has the predominant number of enrollments in the ANZ segment) has an allotment of 2,770 visas. This suggests to me that new incoming international students were likely down at least 20-25% YoY.

Once a university reaches 80% of a quota that the government assigns to them, it becomes harder and slower for students to get visas. The overall cap for visas in 2025 is 270K, and the cap in 2026 is 295K.

These rules are likely in place for the foreseeable future. The Labor government will be up for reelection in 2028, and at the moment they are rather popular with a recent landslide victory in the May 2025 election. So these regulations will likely not abate.

Our understanding is that the Australian school year commences in late January/early February and that most of the international students attending Torrens were traditional students rather than working adult students. Which means that from a visa perspective, presumably most of the international students have received the visas that they need.

That being said, management said something rather ominous in the Q4 2024 call regarding the full impact of the visa cap for the 2H25 that does raise questions regarding risk. CEO Karl McDonnell said that “enrollment up until you hit that cap, those visas would be approved kind of at normal speed, and that's where we're at since we're at the very beginning of the year. It wouldn't be until later in the year, as our enrollment starts to buttress up against that cap number, that we may start to see the visa slow down.”

The CEO's statement that visa approvals may slow "later in the year" as enrollments approach the cap conflicts with my understanding of the international student intake, which primarily occurs in January/February. This discrepancy raises uncertainty for me about the timing of visa-related impacts.

Can management's focus on enrolling domestic students offset known weakness with international students? As management said on the 2Q25 earnings conference call, "Notwithstanding the recent decline in our international enrollment, we are optimistic about our pivot to focusing primarily on the Australian domestic market, where we have seen mid- to high single-digit new student growth through the first half of this year."

Results for Q3 and Q4 in ANZ are potentially concerning. However, management's pivot on the domestic side has demonstrated that it can attract mid- to high-enrollment growth YoY. If it can replicate this next year, then this bodes well for the company's overall revenue growth in 2026.

Valuation

Strategic Education’s valuation multiples have compressed over the past year, mirroring the revenue growth deceleration experienced by the company and the uncertainty regarding the magnitude of challenges in ANZ. It’s worth noting that STRA’s P/E ratio of 13.7x is 40% lower than the S&P 500’s forward P/E ratio of 23x.

I value STRA at 20x P/E, still a discount to the S&P 500. $125/share, representing close to 50% upside.

It’s conceivable to do a sum of the parts of STRA’s three segments. However, the ETS business serves as a significant funnel for enrollments to Strayer and Capella Universities. It’s unclear how a transaction would be structured that would maintain the enterprise value of USHE and ETS.

Conclusion

Strategic Education’s Q2 2025 struggles, driven by ANZ visa caps and USHE enrollment dips, are, in my opinion, temporary within the company's long-term notional model. ANZ’s domestic focus, ETS’s strength, and USHE’s stability set the stage for 2026 growth. Trading at a significant discount and offering a dividend double that of the S&P 500, STRA offers a compelling risk-reward profile for investors looking past near-term hurdles.

data.title: Alibaba: Market Is Overestimating Market For AI Cloud - Downgrade To Strong Sell
data.description:
Alibaba (NYSE:BABA) has surged as the market appears to be warming up to the name as a potential AI winner. I view this enthusiasm as being misplaced, as investors appear to be overestimating the addressable market for the cloud operations. The company maintains a strong balance sheet and might not look that expensive at first glance, but investors may be forgetting the inherent geopolitical and corporate structure risks. I see fair value as being significantly lower than current stock prices, justifying my downgrade to a strong sell rating.

BABA Stock Price

I last covered BABA in March, where I downgraded the stock to a sell rating when the stock had soared during the emergence of DeepSeek (remember this?). The stock at one point did dip but as of late had returned 25% since then.

The stock valuation is now looking eerily similar to that of the US mega-cap tech names, which I view to be a clear warning sign of an overheated market.

BABA Stock Key Metrics

BABA is a Chinese tech conglomerate best known for its e-commerce operations. It was one of the first movers in the e-commerce space, making it once known as the “Amazon of China,” but competition has emerged since. The most recent quarter saw the company show tepid top-line growth, with non-GAAP earnings seeing double-digit declines.

Management attributed most of that disappointing outcome as being due to the dispositions of Sun Art and Intime. Management noted that revenue excluding these two dispositions jumped 10% YoY. The company saw 10% YoY growth in its e-commerce operations, though e-commerce EBITDA declined due to its investment in its “Taobao Instant Commerce” product. The cloud business saw some strength, with revenue growth accelerating to 26% YoY.

The company maintained a strong balance sheet highlighted by $49.4 billion in net cash.

The company repurchased $815 million of stock in the quarter. The company has greatly slowed down its share repurchase program in part due to growing CapEx needs.

On the conference call, management noted that “Customer management revenue of our e-commerce business increased by 10%, primarily driven by the improvement of take rate.” This is an important datapoint as customer management revenue made up 64% of overall e-commerce revenue. Take rate increases are inherently less sustainable than growth through gross merchandise volume (‘GMV’) and thus I expect e-commerce growth to slow down moving forward once the company begins lapping tough comparables.

Is BABA Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

BABA stock has been on a roll lately. This appears to be largely due to news that the company would be ramping up its investments in artificial intelligence spending. I understand why there’s increased interest in the stock, as investors may be looking for the next big winner in AI with US equity valuations looking overheated. That said, I still do not agree with the magnitude of enthusiasm being placed here.

For starters, cloud revenues made up just a fraction of the overall business. This is an example in which being a conglomerate often sounds better than it actually is.

The most important point is that I am doubtful BABA will be able to grow its cloud business anywhere near what appears to be implied in the stock price. Due to heightened political tensions, it seems unlikely for global customers to move their cloud operations to a Chinese cloud provider. While BABA does have global cloud customers, it appears that many of these use cases are primarily due to address specifically Chinese operations. I made that conclusion based on filtering by country on their “customer success” page.

That brings us to valuation. After the big rally, BABA found itself trading at around 22x earnings.

As previously noted, the company has $49.4 billion in net cash. The company also has another $86 billion in equity securities, other investments, and investments in equity method investees. These still look significant relative to the $380 billion market cap. That said, BABA has not shown evidence of willingness to use these items in any meaningful way towards share repurchases. Bullish investors might seek to calculate the valuation “net of cash and investments,” but I caution against such analysis.

At these valuations, BABA is trading just barely lower than the 25x earnings multiple at Alphabet (GOOGL) and the 27x earnings multiple at Meta Platforms (META), to name a few. I find that relative discount to be inadequate, as investors appear to be forgetting the reasons why the stock previously traded at larger discounts.

Besides the ongoing geopolitical tensions (not to mention an in-progress battle of tariffs), it bears reminding that BABA is not structured like your typical stock in the United States. BABA is a variable interest entity (‘VIE’) which means that investors in BABA do not actually own Alibaba but instead own ownership in a company which owns contracts giving it ownership of the underlying earnings of the Alibaba company. This complicated structure is due to the fact that the Chinese government technically prohibits foreign investors from owning stakes in certain Chinese businesses.

In my view, this complicated structure elevates shareholder protection risks which in conjunction with ongoing geopolitical tensions, justifies a more material relative discount. Absent that discount, the market appears to be expecting the cloud business to see an extreme acceleration in growth. However as previously noted, I do not expect this acceleration to be that significant given that the company is likely limited primarily to Chinese customers and the cloud business is still rather small relative to the overall business. I see BABA eventually trading down to 10x to 13x earnings, representing a stock price of between $79 to $102 per share.

BABA Stock Conclusion

One might be hoping that BABA is the next big AI winner, but I struggle to see how the cloud business can grow to the extent that investors are hoping for. Share repurchases have dwindled in recent quarters due to ongoing investments in CapEx and I fear that investors may be forgetting why the stock previously traded at deep discounts. I recommend investors to instead look for undervalued opportunities among quality names, as I have done in the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio. I am downgrading the stock to a strong sell rating.

data.title: The U.S. Government Shutdown Is Looming
data.description:
As we approach the September 30, 2025, fiscal year deadline, the possibility of a U.S. government shutdown keeps increasing, as Congress shows almost no progress on budget negotiations. The risk of a government shutdown during budget negotiations has become a routine feature of America’s budget process, though there are plenty of reasons to be cautious this time.

At the same time, I think any shutdown-related market weakness should be used as a buying opportunity for investors, so I keep a constructive view of the U.S. stock market.

Historical Context: Learning From Past Shutdowns

If we look at the historical data, we can notice that the longest shutdown in U.S. history happened under Trump's first administration, lasting 23 days from December 21, 2018, to January 14, 2019. This exceeded the previous record of 21 days under Clinton in December 1995. What's particularly interesting is that under Trump, there were three shutdowns during his first term: the aforementioned 23-day shutdown, a 2-day shutdown in January 2018, and a 1-day shutdown in February 2018.

As for the market performance, during Trump's longest shutdown, the S&P 500 fell dramatically by 7.05% in the week prior to the shutdown ending and declined 8.80% in the month before resolution. This was the worst performance among all shutdowns since 1976. However, it's important to note that this period coincided with broader market volatility related to Federal Reserve tightening and US-China “trade war 1.0” concerns, so the shutdown wasn't the only factor affecting markets back then.

In contrast, market performance during the majority of other shutdowns was relatively modest. The average S&P 500 performance one week before shutdown endings was -0.23%, while the median was actually positive at 0.15%. During the one-month period before shutdowns ended, the average return was 0.06% with a median of 0.73%. About 55% of shutdowns saw positive returns in the week before ending, and 60% showed gains in the month before resolution.

Moreover, in the longer term, the effect of shutdowns on market performance is minimal. Out of 22 instances of a government shutdown, in 19 cases, the 12-month performance of the S&P 500 after the end of a shutdown was positive.

In my opinion, that's a good example of why investors should maintain their long-term focus even when dramatic market events occur.

How Likely Is The Shutdown This Time?

To avoid the shutdown, Congress needs to pass either a short-term funding bill, called a continuing resolution (or CR), or they must approve 12 separate full-year funding bills. However, considering that Congress is limited on time to finish work on the full-year funding bills before the deadline, they’ll need to pass a short-term funding bill.

The most recent attempt to avoid the shutdown last week was unsuccessful: Democrats blocked the bill from passing the Senate and instead offered their own version of the bill that included a number of health care provisions Democrats claim to be essential. In response, Republicans blocked that bill in the Senate.

In the meantime, Trump canceled a meeting with Democratic leaders to negotiate the bill, dismissing Democrats' fiscal demands. Again, based on recent history, it's a normal thing that both sides keep escalating the situation before reaching a "last-minute solution", though statistics from Trump's first term don't play in favor of a swift resolution of the current confrontation between Republicans and Democrats this time.

The Market Outlook

The S&P 500 keeps trading near all-time highs, which makes it somewhat vulnerable to potential market volatility caused by the shutdown.

Despite the fact there's less than a week left until the deadline, market participants seem to be completely unworried at this point. The VIX indicator stays near its yearly lows, and what I find concerning is how quickly the VIX can spike from these compressed levels.

As we see from the chart, we've had several mini-spikes reaching the 25-30 range even after the initial panic subsided. These episodic volatility bursts show that investors' sentiment can shift rapidly, especially from such low levels.

Speaking of sentiment, the lack of extreme bullish sentiment implies limited potential impact of any serious market stress. Given that investors don't seem to feel euphoric, that makes a sudden shock less likely to trigger a self-reinforcing market drop.

Therefore, I don't expect a total market meltdown in case of a government shutdown, but a combination of a low VIX and the S&P 500 trading near all-time highs is still a suitable setup for a market correction caused by an external shock.

The Bottom Line

While government shutdowns have historically demonstrated limited market impact, the current political context makes this potential shutdown a bit more concerning than usual. If we don’t see Congress passing the bill anytime soon, I expect market volatility to increase dramatically. At the same time, based on historical patterns showing that long-term performance of stocks after shutdowns is usually positive, any noticeable weakness should be used to buy the dip.

data.title: VIG: A Sleep Easy At Night Investment Choice
data.description:
Introduction

It's been almost ten months since my initial coverage of the Vanguard Dividend Appreciation Index Fund ETF Shares (NYSEARCA:VIG). The fund's name is a bit of a mouthful, so I will refer to it simply as VIG from here on. The ETF has delivered a total return of close to 8% since my initial coverage, which in isolation is a decent return. Although it does trail the S&P 500 over that same time frame. However, given the S&P 500 is not the ideal benchmark for a dividend growth ETF and the fact market performance has been so concentrated in mega-cap tech names, I retain my core conviction in VIG as an investment vehicle.

Since my last time of writing, Vanguard has even managed to lower the expense ratio on VIG from 0.06% to 0.05%. Now I know a single basis point is meaningless, but for me it's the principle of the matter that's important here. VIG was already a best-in-class offering from a cost perspective, and yet Vanguard is still striving to deliver truly the lowest-cost option for investors. Since the firm's founding and the work of the legendary Jack Bogle, the objective has always been to minimize the fee load for ordinary investors, allowing them to participate in financial markets without being fleeced in the process. That mission continues strong to this day; it's good to see.

Anything can happen in markets in the short term. Price movements so often are driven by the gyrations of investor sentiment and mood. When we zoom out, though, the evidence of a high-quality investment should be clear; it should jump off the chart at you. A zoomed-out view of VIG makes it clear as day that this is an excellent investment choice in which to compound wealth. Many investors enjoy the thrill of stock picking, the dutiful study of businesses, the following of their earnings and news. For many other investors, all they want is a safe and reliable fund that will allow them to rest easy at night and confidently save for the future.

It is for this second class of investor that VIG is so nicely suited. I reiterate a strong buy rating on this top tier ETF, I see it as a wonderful choice for those individuals who want steady returns, minimal effort, and an easy-to-track broker statement. VIG combines that lovely blend of earnings growth, dividends, low cost track record, and quality of issuing institution.

Performance

When we take a long-term snapshot of VIG since its inception, it looks as though this ETF never did anything except go straight up. Of course, over shorter time periods, there were plenty of moments to give investors pause, as short-term concerns about this or that headline spooked the market. I think there is good value in taking a long-term view, as it can help to remind us that the vast majority of day-to-day news flow is simply noise and should not influence our decision-making.

The long-term performance also looks attractive compared to the S&P 500. Since its inception, VIG has outperformed the index by more than 130%, which is quite a delta.

In terms of short-term price action, my recommendation at the end of last year was somewhat unfortunately timed, as shares were close to an all-time high. Coming into 2025, VIG suffered a sharp decline into April along with the broader market. The ETF did not break through the prior ATH until June. The fund has rallied since April but not to the same extent as the overall market, an explainable difference given the strength and index weight of names like Nvidia (NVDA) which VIG does not hold.

Portfolio

The VIG portfolio holds a lot of names that will be familiar to investors. It is noteworthy that despite this being a dividend fund, it is focused also on growth companies of high quality. A look at the top ten holdings shows lots of really great companies, many of which are dividend payers but maybe not names investors think of when they think of a "dividend stock."

The ability to capture both dividend income and capital appreciation has been key to the success of VIG over time. This is evidenced by the total return performance of the fund vs the broader market since inception. The average earnings growth of the fund is close to 13%, which is a really attractive level of growth, and ultimately it is earnings growth that underpins sustainable dividend growth going forward. I liked the characteristics of the fund when I covered it last year, and my perspective has not changed. This fund is a great low-cost way to get access to a diversified basket of high-quality stocks, delivering excellent growth and returning cash to shareholders as dividends and, importantly, growing those dividends over time. A wonderful combination that gives confidence in an attractive long-term total return profile.

Expenses

As I said in my opening section, the expense profile of VIG is unrivaled. There isn't a huge amount more I can say about it, really. VIG is an instrument that has outperformed the S&P 500 since its inception by over 100%, and yet we pay a mere 5 basis points for the privilege of being investors. The investment world is littered with expensive ETFs, which charge investors unnecessary fees that do not add any value.

The fact that Vanguard lowered the expense ratio to 5 basis points from 6 at my last time of writing signals to me there is an enduring commitment to delivering low cost to investors. From an investment return perspective, let's be honest, a single basis point makes no difference. It is the principle of the matter that's important here, Vanguard's reputation was built on low-cost indexing, they are certainly staying true to form.

Risks

The risks here are much the same as they are for the broader market. In the short term there are concerns about the state of the US labor market. Indications from recent non-farms indicate a sharply weakening labor market, which could potentially presage a coming recession. Along with that, we have a Federal Reserve grappling with inflation persistently above target yet faced with signs of a weaker jobs market. A conundrum for the dual mandate. There is the risk the Fed remains hawkish too long on monetary policy, a recession becomes evident with a disastrous labor print, and all of a sudden we are in an emergency rate cut situation. Such a situation would be damaging for stocks across the board in the short term.

On the flip side, you have a market that's been driven by AI and data center spending. Huge sums of money are floating around, but there are signs the ROI proposition might not be there. A recent MIT study suggested companies are essentially seeing no benefit yet from AI. That might be because there is simply a lag between AI tools being developed and companies adopting them for suitable use cases in their business. Or it could be that LLMs are great tools for consumers to chat with or use as a stand-in therapist, but they have little business value. If the latter, then we are facing down a situation where billions upon billions have been spent with no ROI ever possible to recover.

Once again, if this situation comes to pass, in the near term stocks are going to struggle. Luckily for VIG investors, the fund is not over-indexed to AI names, though it does hold some notable leaders such as Broadcom (AVGO). As I hope I effectively highlighted in my earlier analysis, over the long term VIG is a fund that has delivered dependable growth. The short-term price action can and will be choppy at times, investors should take a long-term view with VIG and look at the zoomed out chart as a visual reminder of how small the short-term noise is when you look at it through that lens.

Conclusion

VIG is a great ETF, a great choice for the investor who wants a simple, low-cost total portfolio solution. The fund delivers a great blend of growth and dividends at a cost that is the best in the market and is a trusted provider of great estimation. I reiterate a strong buy on the fund. It hasn't performed quite as well as the broader market since my initial coverage nearly ten months ago. However, it has delivered close to an 8% total return since then, which in the grand scheme of things, is a solid return for just a ten month period. This one is a keeper in my view, buy it, hold it, let it compound.
